stance_summary: Forbidden anchor (extraction/normalization of S from a correct wavefunction) is set aside on purpose. The η≈1-for-everything symptom is exactly what you get when the rotated 3-body BREAKUP continuum is not physically PRESENT in the solved object ψ_sc — not when a present continuum is mis-read. If the rotated continuum genuinely carried ~54% of the flux, NO amplitude-bookkeeping bug could turn η=0.46 into η≈1.16; a Jacobian/C_n error rescales |S| but does not manufacture unitarity. So the defect is upstream: in what the operator A, the source b, and the complex rotation actually build. These three candidates each interrogate generation, not read-out, and each comes with a diagnostic that needs NO amplitude formula at all.
anchored_assumption: extraction-normalization (forbidden) — these candidates instead test source/operator/CS-solution generation of breakup flux

candidates.one_line: θ-plateau diagnostic on the RAW solution norm/flux, no amplitude formula: a correct CS calc has δ,η on a plateau in θ∈[4,12.5]° with η<1; flat η≈1 across ALL θ (incl. large θ near the 14.2° limit) proves the rotated continuum is absent from ψ_sc, not mis-extracted.
candidates.first_concrete_step: In benchmark_nd_scatt.jl run_point, scan θ_deg∈{4,6,8,10,12} at FIXED reduced mesh (nx=12,ny=30,xmax=24,ymax=50), DOUBLET E_lab=14.1. For each θ record (a) the current δ,η AND (b) two extraction-free quantities: the interior breakup weight W_brk = Σ over all NON-deuteron channels of ⟨ψ_sc|B|ψ_sc⟩ restricted to those channels, and the deuteron-channel weight W_d likewise. Plot δ(θ), η(θ), and W_brk/W_d(θ). Interpretation: a true CS solution gives a δ,η PLATEAU and a θ-stable, nonzero W_brk/W_d; if δ,η drift monotonically with θ and W_brk≈0 for every θ, the continuum is simply not being generated (kills extraction as the culprit). 1-day: pure driver-level loop + a 5-line norm reducer, ~5×(tens of s) reduced-mesh runs.
candidates.cost_estimate: ~1 day; 5 reduced-mesh runs (~minutes total) + tiny norm-reduction helper. No new physics code.

info_gain.branch_plateau_with_eta_lt_1: If even at reduced mesh there is a θ-window where η dips below 1 and δ/η flatten, then the continuum IS generated and the production-mesh η≈1.16 is a convergence/extraction artifact after all — REINSTATES the forbidden anchor as the lead and tells you to push mesh, not physics.
info_gain.branch_flat_eta_1_and_W_brk_zero: If η≈1 and W_brk≈0 for ALL θ, the rotated breakup continuum carries no probability in ψ_sc — the bug is in GENERATION (operator/source/rotation), validating A6/A7 and definitively exonerating extraction.

candidates.risk: Reduced mesh may be too coarse to resolve any plateau even when physics is right (Lazauskas warns CS wants 30–40 splines/dir); a flat result at nx=12 is suggestive, not conclusive. Mitigate by also running θ=10° at production mesh once for the W_brk number.

orthogonality_to_tried.difference: T1 fixed a read-out coefficient on a single deuteron component; A5 never forms the amplitude — it weighs the raw ψ_sc by channel via B and varies θ. A unitarity-from-construction test, not a recoupling test.
T2 is about which deuteron components enter the channel-spin recoupling; A5 looks at the NON-deuteron (breakup) channels' weight, which T2 never touched.
T3 changed one scalar in A at fixed θ=10°; A5 holds the energy convention and instead asks whether the answer is θ-stationary at all — a property T3 cannot reveal at a single θ.
A4/T4 is purely inside the amplitude inner product; A5 deletes the amplitude from the test entirely and reads channel norms.
T5 toggled conjugation in the read-out; A5's W_brk uses the same conjugated B-metric the bound state uses and never enters the amplitude.

orthogonality_to_tried.tried_item: T6 (no B metric in amplitude ME)
orthogonality_to_tried.difference: T6 settled a metric convention for the amplitude; A5 deliberately USES B (it is a probability/flux weight, the legitimate place for B) on the raw solution.

T7 scanned CHANNEL count at fixed θ=10° and found δ,η identical; A5 scans θ at fixed channels and reads breakup-channel WEIGHT, a different axis and a different observable that T7's identical-δ result actually makes more suspicious (extra channels present but carrying zero weight ⇒ continuum not fed).
T8 confirmed Aψ=b is solved to 2e-5; A5 takes that converged ψ as given and asks whether its breakup content is physical vs θ — orthogonal to residual size.

candidates.dependencies: Needs a per-channel ⟨ψ|B|ψ⟩ reducer (B is already built in compute_scattering_matrix; channel indexing i=(iα-1)*nx*ny+(ix-1)*ny+iy is documented). No new solves beyond the θ loop.
candidates.differs_from_forbidden: Produces δ,η,W_brk WITHOUT ever calling compute_scattering_amplitude or any C_n/Jacobian/prefactor — η here is read as |S| only for plateau shape; the decisive number W_brk is a raw norm. It tests presence of flux, not its read-out.

candidates.one_line: Audit whether the INCOMING source wave φ_in is built so flux can leak to breakup: F_λ is currently evaluated at the ROTATED argument k·y·e^{iθ} (so ψ_in itself decays like exp(−ky sinθ)), but the physical incoming channel must be the UN-rotated regular wave; a rotated source injects a decaying, square-integrable driver that the operator can fully re-absorb elastically → η→1.
candidates.first_concrete_step: Read matrices_optimized.jl:1244-1259: y_scaled = yi*exp(iθ), x_coulomb = k*y_scaled, F_λ from COULCC at that complex argument. Build a one-cell unit test: at θ=10°, E_lab=14.1, plot |ψ_in(y)| along y for a deuteron channel. Lazauskas: the SCATTERED part decays exp(−kr sinθ); the INCOMING part Ψ^in feeding V·Ψ^in as the source should be the ordinary regular function so V·Ψ^in is a localized (short-range) source, while the response ψ_sc is what decays. Check sign/placement: is the code rotating the source when it should leave the incoming free wave real-argument and let ONLY ψ_sc decay? Test by recomputing b with F_λ(k·y) (un-rotated argument) while keeping the operator A scaled, and compare η. 1-day: a flag in compute_initial_state_vector to skip the y-rotation of the COULCC argument, two reduced-mesh runs.
candidates.cost_estimate: ~1 day; 1 small flag + 2 reduced-mesh runs + one |ψ_in(y)| plot.

info_gain.branch_eta_drops: If un-rotating the COULCC argument in the source makes η drop below 1, the source was a decaying square-integrable driver that the elastic channel trivially re-absorbed — the breakup continuum was never DRIVEN. Direct hit on generation.
info_gain.branch_no_change: If η stays ≈1, the source argument is not the issue and the rotation of the incoming wave was correct (Lazauskas inhomogeneous form does feed V·Ψ^in with Ψ^in built consistently under CS); attention moves to A7 (operator-side consistency of T vs V vs the source).

candidates.risk: The Lazauskas inhomogeneous form Ψ=Ψ^in+Ψ^sc may legitimately require the rotated Ψ^in (so V·Ψ^in is the genuine source); naively un-rotating could break the asymptotic match and is physically wrong if so. Must check against Lazauskas Eq. for the source, not just empirically — physics-first, treat the empirical η drop as a CLUE to be justified, not a fix to keep blindly.

orthogonality_to_tried.difference: T1 is post-solve recoupling; A6 changes the RHS b that drives the solve — upstream of every read-out fix.
T2 concerns deuteron-component bookkeeping in extraction; A6 concerns the radial free-wave argument k·y·e^{iθ} in the source, untouched by T2.
T3 changed the operator energy; A6 leaves E_total alone and changes how φ_in's free wave is built — different equation term (b vs A).
T4 scaled V in the read-out; A6 questions the F_λ argument in the source, a different object.
T5 is an inner-product convention in extraction; A6 is about whether the driver wave physically excites breakup — no inner product involved.
T6 is amplitude-metric; A6 is source construction. Disjoint.

T7 added channels but the source only ever populates deuteron-coupling channels (lines 1227-1231); A6 asks whether the source even drives flux OUT of those into the breakup channels T7 added — explains T7's null directly.
T8 verified A·ψ=b; A6 changes b itself, so a converged solve of a WRONG source is exactly the failure mode T8 cannot see.

candidates.dependencies: compute_initial_state_vector (matrices_optimized.jl:1165) editable for a no-rotate-argument flag; COULCC already accepts complex args. Independent of A5/A7.
candidates.differs_from_forbidden: Operates entirely on the RHS source b=2·V·Rxy_31·φ_in and the construction of φ_in — never on how S is read from ψ_sc. Even the η comparison is a before/after on the source, not a re-derivation of the amplitude formula.

candidates.one_line: Verify the complex rotation is applied CONSISTENTLY across every operator piece that feeds breakup: T carries exp(−2iθ) and V uses backward-rotated φ(r e^{−iθ}), but Rxy (the rearrangement that COUPLES the elastic channel to breakup) is built θ-independently — if Rxy is geometrically θ-free but the V it multiplies is rotated, the V·Rxy coupling that should bleed flux into the rotated continuum may be evaluated at inconsistent radial arguments, decoupling breakup.
candidates.first_concrete_step: Confirm at scattering.jl:105 Rxy_matrix_optimized(α,grid) takes NO θ. Decide the physics: Rxy is a pure coordinate-permutation overlap (kinematic, θ-independent in principle) — BUT the radial Laguerre basis functions it is expressed in are the SAME basis whose arguments V rotates. Unit test: form the product (V_scaled · Rxy) and (T_scaled) at θ=10° and at θ=0, and check the off-diagonal (deuteron-channel)×(breakup-channel) blocks of V·(I+Rxy): do they carry the expected exp(−iθ)-type rotation, or are they real/θ-frozen because Rxy injects an unrotated radial dependence? Concretely: print the ratio of the deuteron↔breakup coupling block norm at θ=10° vs θ=0; if the ratio is ≈1 (block unchanged by rotation) while the within-channel V block clearly rotated, the coupling term is inconsistent and the breakup continuum sees a non-rotated coupling that cannot absorb rotated outgoing flux. 1-day: two matrix builds (θ=0 and θ=10°) at reduced mesh + a block-norm comparison; no solve needed.
candidates.cost_estimate: ~1 day; 2 reduced-mesh matrix assemblies (no GMRES solve) + block-norm script.

info_gain.branch_coupling_block_frozen: If the deuteron↔breakup coupling block is θ-invariant while T and within-channel V rotate, the rotation is inconsistent across the coupling operator — the continuum is rotated but its FEED is not, so flux cannot transfer. Points to making Rxy's radial representation CS-consistent (e.g. rotate the Laguerre arguments inside Rxy, or absorb e^{iθ} Jacobians into the V·Rxy product as the operator demands).
info_gain.branch_coupling_block_rotates: If the coupling block does rotate consistently, the operator is CS-coherent and the defect is not operator-side — combined with A5/A6 nulls this would push back toward a deep mesh/continuum-resolution issue (Lazauskas' 30–40 splines/dir warning) rather than a code bug.

candidates.risk: Rxy genuinely IS θ-independent as a geometric operator, so a θ-frozen coupling block could be CORRECT physics, not a bug; the discriminating test is whether the COMBINATION V·Rxy (and the source V·Rxy_31·φ) carries the rotation V should impart. Risk of mis-diagnosing a correct geometric Rxy as a bug — must reason at the product level, not Rxy alone.

orthogonality_to_tried.difference: T1 is angular recoupling in extraction; A7 is radial CS-consistency of the operator coupling block — different sector entirely.
T2 deuteron-component count in read-out; A7 inspects breakup-coupling operator blocks pre-solve.
T3 tuned the diagonal E·B term; A7 examines the off-diagonal V·(I+Rxy) coupling rotation — orthogonal term of A.
T4 scaled V only in the post-solve amplitude; A7 checks whether V·Rxy IN THE OPERATOR A is CS-consistent — same V but in the solve, not the read-out.
T5 inner-product convention; A7 operator block-norm comparison. Disjoint.
T6 amplitude metric; A7 operator assembly. Disjoint.

T7 found extra channels DECOUPLE from the elastic observable — A7 supplies the mechanism: if the V·Rxy coupling block to those channels is θ-frozen/inconsistent, they cannot couple, which is precisely T7's symptom seen from the operator side.
T8 checked the solve of A; A7 checks whether A itself is CS-coherent in its coupling block — a converged solve of a coupling-inconsistent A is the gap T8 leaves.

candidates.dependencies: Rxy_matrix_optimized (matrices_optimized.jl:182, θ-free) and V_matrix_optimized_scaled (line 663) already exist; only a block-norm comparison harness is new. No solve, so cheapest of the three. Independent of A5/A6 but A7's null + A5's null together would strongly indict mesh resolution rather than code.

convergence_note: All three deliberately violate the extraction-normalization anchor and instead localize the η≈1 defect to GENERATION of breakup flux: A5 measures whether flux is present in ψ_sc (raw channel norms vs θ, no amplitude formula), A6 whether the source DRIVES it (RHS free-wave argument), A7 whether the operator COUPLES it (CS-consistency of V·Rxy). Run order by cost/decisiveness: A7 first (no solve, cheapest, directly tests the coupling that T7 showed is dead), then A5 (θ-plateau + W_brk, the model-independent presence test), then A6 (source argument) only if A5 shows W_brk≈0. A5's W_brk and A7's coupling-block-norm are the two extraction-free numbers that can falsify the forbidden anchor outright: if both show the continuum is genuinely absent from the solved object, no amount of Eq.16/C_n/Jacobian bookkeeping can be the cause. Not picking a winner.
